---
title: "galton-height-regression"
output: html_document
---

# Title

## Summary

## Introduction

## Methods

To investigate the relationship between parental height and child height, we conducted a mutiple linear regression analysis using the Galton height dataset. The dataset was randomly split into training (80%) and testing (20%) sets. The training dataset was used to fit the regression model, while the testing dataset was used to evaluate the model's predictive performance.

A multiple linear regression model was fitted with child height as the response variable, and the midparent height (the average height of the mother and father) and child gender as predictor variables.

## Analysis

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)

set.seed(123)

# Load data
galton_heights <- read_csv("data/processed/galton_clean.csv")

# Split the data into training (80%) and testing (20%) sets
galton_split <- initial_split(galton_heights, prop = 0.8)

galton_train <- training(galton_split)
galton_test  <- testing(galton_split)

# Specify the linear regression model
lm_spec <- linear_reg() |>
  set_engine("lm") |>
  set_mode("regression")

# Define the model formula 
lm_recipe <- recipe(
  child_height ~ midparent_height + gender,
  data = galton_train)

# Build workflow and fit the model
lm_fit <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(lm_recipe) |>
  add_model(lm_spec) |>
  fit(data = galton_train)

# View model summary
lm_fit

# Extract the regression coefficients
coeffs <- lm_fit |>
             extract_fit_parsnip() |>
             tidy()

# View coefficients
coeffs

# Predict on the test data and evaluate model performance
lm_test_results <- lm_fit |>
  predict(galton_test) |>
  bind_cols(galton_test) |>
  metrics(truth = child_height, estimate = .pred)

# View test results
lm_test_results

# Scatterplot with regression line
ggplot(galton_heights, aes(x = midparent_height, y = child_height, color = gender)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  labs(
    x = "Midparent Height (inches)",
    y = "Child Height (inches)",
    title = "Graph 1. Relationship Between Midparent Height and Child Height")
```

## Results

The fitted regression model produced the following equation:

$$
\text{Child Height} = 16.14 + 0.693(\text{Midparent Height}) + 5.21(\text{Male})
$$ 

The results indicate that midparent height is a significant predictor of child height (β = 0.693, p \< 0.001). For every one inch increase in midparent height, the predicted child height increases by approximately 0.69 inches.

Child gender was also a significant predictor (β = 5.21, p \< 0.001). Male children were predicted to be approximately 5.21 inches taller than female children on average, when holding the midparent height constant.

The model achieved a root mean squared error (RMSE) of 2.28 inches and a mean absolute error (MAE) of 1.76 inches, indicating that predictions were typically within approximately 2 inches of the true child height. The model explained 61.6% of the variation in child height (R² = 0.616).

Graph 1 shows the relationship between midparent height and child height, with separate regression lines for male and female children. The positive slope for both lines indicates that taller parents tend to have taller children. The colour separation between male and female points in the scatterplot reflects the gender difference, with male children generally having greater predicted heights than female children at the same midparent height.

## Discussion

## References
